title: Our Passport To The World
objectives: Identify travel and airport vocabulary in listening tasks
Use present perfect with 'just' to describe recent actions (have/has + just + past participle)
Read and comprehend passages about air traffic controllers and world exploration
Use prepositions of time (at, in, on) correctly
Recognise silent 'w' in wr- words and the -tion suffix for verb→noun conversion
Write structured paragraphs using graphic organisers and Venn diagrams

vocabulary.definition: A place where planes take off and land
To feel anxious or concerned about something
The desk at an airport where you show your ticket and passport before flying
An official document that allows you to travel to other countries
A card that lets you get on the plane, showing your seat number and flight
A journey by plane from one place to another
A place to sit, especially on a plane or bus
The waiting area in an airport where passengers wait before boarding
When a plane comes down and touches the ground
A person who works in a tall tower at an airport and gives planes instructions for safe take-off, landing, and movement
A direction or order telling someone what to do
When a plane leaves the ground and starts flying
One of the seven large land areas of the world (Asia, Africa, Europe, North America, South America, Australia, Antarctica)
The area in an airport where passengers go after their plane lands
In a way that shows you believe in yourself and your abilities
The area in an airport where passengers collect their bags after a flight
The place you are travelling to
To cover something by putting paper or cloth around it (silent w)
A business that provides a service, like a travel agency
The flat surface of a computer, phone, or TV that shows images
A season of very heavy rain, especially in Asia
A small number of; fewer is used for comparing
A series of names, items, or things written down
Without being controlled or limited; in a free way
To become aware of something; to understand
Relating to the tropics — hot and humid areas near the equator
To welcome someone when you meet them
An ability to do something well, learned through practice

key_concepts.explanation: Structure: Subject + have/has + just + past participle. Use 'have' with I/you/we/they. Use 'has' with he/she/it. Negative: haven't/hasn't + just + past participle. 'Just' means a very short time ago. Examples: 'Ali has just lost his passport.' 'They have just found a book.' 'The plane has just landed.' Irregular past participles: take→taken, drive→driven, buy→bought, pay→paid, read→read, land→landed.

AT: specific times (at 10:00, at the moment, at dinnertime, at sunset, at noon). IN: months, years, centuries, parts of the day, long periods (in summer, in May, in the 1960s, in 1985, in the 20th century, in afternoon). ON: days, dates, parts of a specific day (on Tuesday, on my birthday, on June 15th, on National Day, on Monday evening).

Reading passage: An air traffic controller works in a tall tower at the airport. They give airplanes safe instructions for taking off, landing, and moving. They clear the way for planes, keep flights safe across the seven continents, and guide planes when entering or leaving Kuwait by watching the weather and talking to pilots. After a long flight, passengers walk through the arrival lounge, collect bags from the baggage hall, and meet families.

Reading passage: Noura dreamed of exploring the world. She visited all 7 continents: Asia (learned how people greet in different languages), Europe (walked through old castles), Africa (safari — photographed elephants), North America (rode a bus, enjoyed city views at sunset), South America (tasted tropical fruits), Australia (watched kangaroos jumping freely), Antarctica (photos of snowy mountains and blue ice). She realised exploring is exciting and an explorer needs many skills to travel confidently.

Silent 'w' in wr- words: wrap, wrinkles, writer, wrist — don't pronounce the 'w'. The -tion suffix changes verbs to nouns: act→action, select→selection, collect→collection (drop -t, add -tion). For verbs ending in -te: locate→location, celebrate→celebration, educate→education (drop -te, add -tion).

examples.problem: Lesson 2 Grammar: Present perfect with just
solution_steps: Rule: I/you/we/they + have + just + past participle; he/she/it + has + just + past participle
1. Ali has just ............ his passport. (a. lose, b. lost, c. losing)
2. The students ............ just finished the science project. (a. have, b. has, c. are)
examples.answer: 1. b. lost (past participle); 2. a. have (students = they → have)
examples.visual_description: Grammar chart with have/hasn't and has/hasn't paths, irregular verbs table

solution_steps: AT = specific times, IN = months/years/parts of day, ON = days/dates
1. He plays football (at / in) the afternoon.
2. They go swimming (at / on) 5:00 (at / on) Thursday.
3. We celebrate the National Day (in / on) February.
examples.answer: 1. in the afternoon; 2. at 5:00, on Thursday; 3. in February
examples.visual_description: Three colour-coded boxes: blue=at, green=in, orange=on with examples

exercises.problem: Listening: 1. Hamad is travelling with his school football team. 2. The father is guiding Hamad inside the airport. 3. Hamad has checked his passport at the check-in counter. 4. Hamad's seat number on the plane is 21B.
exercises.answer: Listen and determine true/false; then complete the boarding pass details

exercises.problem: Ali has just ............ his passport. (a. lose, b. lost, c. losing)
The students ............ just finished the science project. (a. have, b. has, c. are)
The best title for the Air Traffic Controller passage: (a. The New Destinations, b. The Seven Continents, c. Inside The Airport, d. An Air Traffic Controller)
The underlined word 'they' in line 10 refers to: (a. continents, b. cultures, c. families, d. languages)
Why does the Air Traffic Controller watch the weather?
exercises.answer: To keep the airport working properly and help people reach new destinations safely, confidently, and on time.

exercises.problem: Reorder the letters: r,i,w,t,e = ___; s,t,r,i,w = ___; w,o,n,r,g = ___
Add -tion to change the verb into a noun: 1. The helmet gives you __________ (protect). 2. The __________ (decorate) is colourful.
Fill in the graphic organiser about 'Air Traffic Controller' using guide words: tower-clear-instructions-check-safely-destinations. Then write a paragraph.
exercises.answer: The air traffic controller works in a tall tower. He gives instructions to planes and clears the way for take-off. He checks the weather and guides pilots. He helps people reach their destinations safely.

exercises.problem: Some markets in Asia open at ............. (a. sunset, b. winter, c. midday, d. afternoon)
exercises.answer: Listen and determine from the recording about continents

exercises.problem: He plays football (at / in) the afternoon.
We celebrate the National Day (in / on) February.
In Noura's world adventure, what did she do in Africa?
exercises.answer: She went on a safari and took photos of elephants resting under the sun.

exercises.problem: Fill in the Venn diagram to compare Asia and Australia using guide words: continent-large-weather-speak-interesting-animals. Then write a paragraph.
exercises.answer: Both: continents, interesting, have animals. Asia: largest, many languages, monsoon season. Australia: smaller, kangaroos, English-speaking.

teaching_notes: This unit introduces travel vocabulary and the present perfect with 'just' — a key Grade 5 grammar point. The airport theme is engaging and practical. The Air Traffic Controller reading connects to real-world careers. Noura's world adventure covers all 7 continents — good for geography integration. The Venn diagram and graphic organiser develop higher-order thinking skills. Fun fact: Kuwait's first airport opened in 1928 in Dasma. Pronunciation focus: silent 'w' in wr- words. Spelling focus: -tion suffix to convert verbs to nouns. 28 vocabulary words across 9 sub-lessons.
